questions.text: Which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A File object encapsulates the properties of a file or a path, but does not contain the methods for reading/writing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You can use the PrintWriter class for outputting text to a file.

choices.label: C
choices.text: You can use the Scanner class for reading text from a file.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An input object is also called an input stream.

choices.label: E
choices.text: An output object is also called an output stream.

questions.explanation: Every statement provided in options A through E is factually accurate. Therefore, you should select the answer that includes all of them since none of the individual claims are false.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Text I/O is built upon binary I/O to provide a level of abstraction for character encoding and decoding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Text I/O involves encoding and decoding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Binary I/O does not require conversions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Binary I/O is more efficient than text I/O, because binary I/O does not require encoding and decoding.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Binary files are independent of the encoding scheme on the host machine and thus are portable.

questions.explanation: Each of the statements from A to E is factually accurate, making the combined answer the correct choice. You should select this because every individual point listed is true.

questions.text: Which method can you use to find out the number of the bytes in a file using InputStream?
questions.explanation: You can use the **available()** method, which returns an estimate of the number of bytes that can be read from the input stream without blocking. This allows you to determine the size of the remaining content in the file stream.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All methods in FileInputStream/FileOutputStream are inherited from InputStream/OutputStream.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You can create a FileInputStream/FileOutputStream from a File object or a file name using FileInputStream/FileOutputStream constructors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The return value -1 from the read() method signifies the end of file.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A java.io.FileNotFoundException would occur if you attempt to create a FileInputStream with a nonexistent file.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A java.io.FileNotFoundException would occur if you attempt to create a FileOutputStream with a nonexistent file.

questions.explanation: Choice E is the correct answer because it is the only statement among the options that is false or inaccurate. You should select it because the question specifically asks you to identify which claim is incorrect.

questions.text: To append data to an existing file, use _____________ to construct a FileOutputStream for file out.dat.
questions.explanation: To append data, you must use the `FileOutputStream` constructor that accepts a second boolean argument set to `true`. This specific parameter tells Java to add new data to the end of the file rather than overwriting its existing content.

questions.text:
What does the following code do?

FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("test.dat");

choices.label: A
choices.text: It creates a new file named test.dat if it does not exist and opens the file so you can write to it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It creates a new file named test.dat if it does not exist and opens the file so you can write to it and read from it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It creates a new file named test.dat regardless of whether it exists or not and opens the file so you can write to it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It creates a new file named test.dat regardless of whether it exists or not and opens the file so you can write to it and read from it.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It creates a FileInputStream for test.dat if test.dat exists.

questions.explanation: This code creates a `FileInputStream` object that opens the file named "test.dat" so your program can read its data. It establishes a connection to the file, allowing you to read its contents as a sequence of bytes.

questions.text: Which type of exception occurs when creating a DataInputStream for a nonexistent file?
questions.explanation: When you attempt to create a stream for a nonexistent file, Java throws a `FileNotFoundException`. This exception occurs because the underlying file stream cannot locate the specific file path you provided.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct to create a DataOutputStream to write to a file named out.dat?
questions.explanation: To create a `DataOutputStream`, you must pass a `FileOutputStream` initialized with the filename "out.dat" into its constructor. This nesting allows the `DataOutputStream` to write primitive data types directly to that specific file.

questions.text:
After the following program is finished, how many bytes are written to the file t.dat?

import java.io.*;

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
    DataOutputStream output = new DataOutputStream(
      new FileOutputStream("t.dat"));
    output.writeShort(1234);
    output.writeShort(5678);
    output.close();
  }
}
questions.explanation: Each `writeShort` method call writes 2 bytes to the output stream. Since the program calls this method twice, a total of 4 bytes are written to the file.

questions.text:
After the following program is finished, how many bytes are written to the file t.dat?

import java.io.*;

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
    DataOutputStream output = new DataOutputStream(
      new BufferedOutputStream(FileOutputStream("t.dat")));
    output.writeChar('A');
    output.close();
  }
}
questions.explanation: In Java, the `writeChar` method writes a character as a 2-byte Unicode value. Therefore, writing a single character like 'A' results in exactly 2 bytes being written to the file.

questions.text:
After the following program is finished, how many bytes are written to the file t.dat?

import java.io.*;

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
    DataOutputStream output = new DataOutputStream(
      new FileOutputStream("t.dat"));
    output.writeChars("ABCD");
    output.close();
  }
}
questions.explanation: In Java, the `writeChars` method writes each character of a string as a two-byte Unicode value. Since the string "ABCD" has four characters, the program writes a total of 8 bytes to the file.

questions.text:
After the following program is finished, how many bytes are written to the file t.dat?

import java.io.*;

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
    DataOutputStream output = new DataOutputStream(
      new FileOutputStream("t.dat"));
    output.writeUTFString("ABCD");
    output.close();
  }
}
questions.explanation: The `writeUTF` method (likely intended by `writeUTFString`) writes two bytes to store the length of the string followed by one byte for each character in "ABCD". This results in a total of 6 bytes: 2 bytes for the length header and 4 bytes for the characters themselves.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: All files are stored in binary format. So, all files are essentially binary files.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Text I/O is built upon binary I/O to provide a level of abstraction for character encoding and decoding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encoding and decoding are automatically performed by text I/O.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For binary input, you need to know exactly how data were written in order to read them in correct type and order.

questions.explanation: All of these statements are correct because each one accurately reflects the facts or principles discussed in your material. Therefore, you should select all the options provided as they are all true.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: ObjectInputStream/ObjectOutputStream enables you to perform I/O for objects in addition for primitive type values and strings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Since ObjectInputStream/ObjectOutputStream contains all the functions of DataInputStream/DataOutputStream, you can replace DataInputStream/DataOutputStream completely by ObjectInputStream/ObjectOutputStream.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To write an object x on ObjectOutputStream output, invoke output.writeObject(x).

choices.label: D
choices.text: To read an object on ObjectInputStream input, invoke input.readObject().

questions.explanation: All of the provided statements are factually correct, so you should select every option. Each choice accurately reflects a different aspect of the topic covered in this lesson.

questions.text:
The Loan class given in the text does not implement java.io.Serializable. Analyze the following code.

public class Foo implements java.io.Serializable {  
  private int v1;
  private static double v2;
  private Loan v3 = new Loan();
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: An instance of Foo can be serialized because Foo implements Serializable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An instance of Foo cannot be serialized because Foo contains a non-serializable instance variable v3.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If you mark v3 as transient, an instance of Foo is serializable.

questions.explanation: Serializing a `Foo` object will throw a `NotSerializableException` because its `v3` field is a `Loan` object, which does not implement `Serializable`. Additionally, the static field `v2` is not included in the serialization process because static members belong to the class rather than individual instances.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A static variable is not serialized.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A transient variable is not serialized.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An object must be an instance of Serializable for it to be serialized.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The methods in an object are serialized.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because it is the only statement that accurately reflects the facts or principles being discussed. You can choose this answer because it correctly identifies the truth among the provided choices.

questions.text: With which I/O class can you append or update a file?
questions.explanation: The `RandomAccessFile` class is correct because it allows you to move to any specific position within a file to both read and write data. This flexibility makes it the ideal tool for either updating existing content or appending new information to the end of a file.

questions.text: Which of the following is the legal mode for creating a new RandomAccessFile stream?
questions.explanation: Choice C is correct because `"rw"` is a legal mode string that allows you to open a `RandomAccessFile` for both reading and writing. In Java, only specific strings such as `"r"`, `"rw"`, `"rws"`, and `"rwd"` are recognized as valid mode arguments.

questions.text:
What happens if the file test.dat does not exist when you attempt to compile and run the following code?

import java.io.*;

class Test  {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      RandomAccessFile raf =
        new RandomAccessFile("test.dat", "r");
      int i = raf.readInt();
    }
    catch(IOException ex) {
      System.out.println("IO exception");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program does not compile because raf is not created correctly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program does not compile because readInt() is not implemented in RandomAccessFile.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program compiles, but throws an IOException because the file test.dat doesn't exist. The program displays IO exception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program compiles and runs fine, but nothing is displayed on the console.

questions.explanation: When you attempt to open a non-existent file in read-only mode ("r"), the program throws a `FileNotFoundException`. Since this is a subclass of `IOException`, the catch block handles the error and prints "IO exception" to the console.

questions.text: To create a file, you can use __________.
questions.explanation: You can use several different classes, such as `File`, `Formatter`, or `PrintWriter`, to create a new file in Java. Option D is correct because it likely includes these multiple valid methods that each allow you to generate a file on your system.
